content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 • 322-5901 322-6441

March 28, 1977

Arthur R. Bluett
1031 Ramirez Street
Marysville, California 95901

Dear Mr. Bluett:

I have been asked to respond to your letter of March 11, 1977. You indicate that you are a candidate for Director of the Olivehurst Public Utility District. You are currently an officer on the Olivehurst Fire Department. You asked whether it is a conflict of interest for a Director of a utility district to be an officer in a fire department within the District.

Generally, it is highly unlikely that any potential conflict of interest situations would arise because of one's service on or employment in two public entities. This is because the Political Reform Act prevents governmental decisions which have an effect upon certain private interests as opposed to public interests. Thus, for instance, the Political Reform Act does not prevent a member of a school board from voting for an increase of salaries for district teachers even though the member's spouse is employed as a teacher within the district. See Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.

I regret that I cannot be more specific in this matter, however, based upon your March 11 letter, I have no additional information. If you require further information regarding this matter, please do not hesitate to call me at 322-6444.

Sincerely,

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

sections.question: You asked whether it is a conflict of interest for a Director of a utility district to be an officer in a fire department within the District.
sections.conclusion: Generally, it is highly unlikely that any potential conflict of interest situations would arise because of one's service on or employment in two public entities. This is because the Political Reform Act prevents governmental decisions which have an effect upon certain private interests as opposed to public interests. Thus, for instance, the Political Reform Act does not prevent a member of a school board from voting for an increase of salaries for district teachers even though the member's spouse is employed as a teacher within the district.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether holding concurrent positions as a Director of the Olivehurst Public Utility District and an officer in the Olivehurst Fire Department constitutes a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that it is highly unlikely a conflict of interest would arise from serving as both a utility district director and fire department officer, as the Political Reform Act only prevents governmental decisions affecting private interests, not decisions affecting the public generally.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a clear FPPC advice letter response dated March 28, 1977. Some OCR artifacts present in original scans but clean version provided. Letter references Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Signed by Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief of Conflicts of Interest Division.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Arthur Bluett's inquiry regarding whether holding concurrent positions as a candidate for Director of the Olivehurst Public Utility District and as an officer in the Olivehurst Fire Department would constitute a conflict of interest. The FPPC concludes such concurrent service is unlikely to create a conflict under the Political Reform Act.
